title-group.article-title: Managing psychological distress in women with breast cancer: A systematic review of intervention trends in the past decade

contrib-group.aff: aDepartment of Health Disparities Research, MD Anderson Cancer Center, Houston, TX, United States
bSchool of Nursing and Research Institute of Nursing Science, Hallym University, Chuncheon, Gangwon-do, Republic of Korea

license.license-p: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/).

sec.title: Objective
sec.p: The rising incidence and survival rates of breast cancer have increased the number of breast cancer survivors (BCSs) experiencing psychological distress that often overlaps with anxiety, depression, and fear of recurrence. Many existing interventions neglect the unique psychosocial contexts of BCSs, and unclear definitions of distress hinder consistent assessment and management. This systematic review evaluated recent interventions targeting psychological distress in BCSs and offers evidence-based insights.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: Following PRISMA guidelines, six databases were searched for randomized controlled trials on distress interventions for BCSs published between 2014 and July 2025. Study quality was evaluated using Cochrane's RoB 2 tool, and data on intervention characteristics, outcomes, and measurement instruments were extracted.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: Fourteen randomized control trials involving 2447 BCSs were included. Among them, 8 (57%) report significant reductions, with six showing immediate post-intervention effects and two demonstrating effects only at follow-up (effect sizes ranged from small to large, Cohen's d = 0.44–1.54). Effective interventions included mindfulness-based approaches, virtual reality-integrated psychotherapy, and cognitive-behavioral therapy combined with hypnosis. In contrast, cognitive-focused interventions lacking experiential components and minimally structured digital programs showed non-significant results.

sec.title: Conclusions
sec.p: Effective distress management requires multimodal, theory-based interventions delivered by trained professionals, rather than cognitive-focused or minimally structured approaches. Future interventions should incorporate standardized distress assessment tools and clearly articulated theoretical frameworks, focusing on younger survivors, who remain significantly underrepresented. Although digital platforms enhance accessibility, their effectiveness depends on evidence-based content and structured implementation. Strengthening these elements will improve both the comparability and overall effectiveness of interventions for BCSs.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: The incidence rate of breast cancer varies across regions and ethnicities, but it consistently increased by more than 1% annually from 2012 to 2021.1 Conversely, the breast cancer mortality rate decreased significantly by 44% in 1989–2022.1 As a result of these trends, the population of women living as breast cancer survivors (BCSs) has grown. With this increasing survivor population, persistent psychological and social challenges, such as anxiety, fear of cancer recurrence, and decreased quality of life, have emerged as crucial concerns throughout their cancer journey.2,3

BCSs experience diverse psychological difficulties that vary throughout their cancer journey. At different stages, they face various psychosocial challenges; among them, psychological distress is a particularly vital issue that encompasses a wide range of negative emotional responses, including anxiety, fear of recurrence, and depression.3 According to the American Psychological Association,4 distress is defined as a negative stress response that often involves negative effects and physiological reactivity because of feeling overwhelmed by demands, losses, or perceived threats. In oncology practice, this general understanding is expanded by the National Comprehensive Cancer Network (NCCN), which specifically defines distress as a multifaceted concept covering physical, psychological, social, or spiritual discomfort that emerges during cancer survivorship, which affects how survivors think, feel, or act.5 This comprehensive definition highlights the complexity and often subtle nature of distress that can remarkably influence survivors’ overall well-being.

Evidence indicates that 20–52% of cancer survivors experience clinically significant distress,5 highlighting its substantial public health effect. This level of distress is often difficult to manage without support.6 Individuals with high distress levels are more likely to use emergency medical services and report greater physical, emotional, familial, and relational difficulties.6 Despite such considerable effects, many cancer survivors report substantial unmet needs for distress management.7 Therefore, nurses play an important role in addressing distress and enhancing the physical and psychological well-being of cancer survivors.

Despite its significance, the concept of distress in BCSs remains ambiguously defined and frequently overlaps with other psychological constructs such as anxiety, depression, and general stress.5 Because of this ambiguity, distress management interventions cannot be effectively evaluated and clinically implemented easily. Previous interventions often addressed multiple psychological elements simultaneously without clearly distinguishing distress from other emotional states; as a result, therapeutic approaches are inconsistent.8 Alanazi et al.9 further highlighted this problem by identifying substantial variations in instruments used to measure psychological distress among BCSs; thus, they revealed the lack of standardized measurement approaches. Such unclear conceptualization complicates the accurate assessment of cancer survivors and reduces the effectiveness of targeted interventions because cancer survivors’ limited illness understanding and uncertainty directly influence assessment accuracy.10

Several systematic reviews of psychological distress interventions for BCSs have been published within the last 5 years, but these studies have limitations in providing evidence for effective distress management for BCSs. The Paley et al.11 study looked at all cancers, making it difficult to identify intervention strategies specific to breast cancer patients, and the Mustilkaningsih et al.,12 Omari et al.,13 and Lin et al.14 studies used psychological distress as the dependent variable, but the actual variables measured included depression, anxiety, and psychological aspects of quality of life. To address these gaps, it is necessary to identify how interventions for psychological distress define their outcomes and what the most current interventions are. In doing so, we hope to provide evidence on what needs to be considered and what directions to take to develop effective psychological distress interventions in the future. Therefore, this systematic review critically evaluates and synthesizes intervention studies published between 2014 and July 2025 to identify and clarify effective approaches for distress management in BCSs. This review aims to provide valuable evidence-based insights and practical recommendations for future clinical practice and research.

sec.title: Study design
sec.p: In this systematic review, managing distress interventions for BCSs was examined. Our results were reported following the suggestions of the Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews (PRISMA) statement.

sec.title: Search methods
sec.p: Six online databases, namely, Embase, CINAHL, PsycINFO, PubMed, Cochrane Library, and Web of Science, were searched for studies published from January 1, 2014, to July 31, 2025. The search was limited to studies published in English. Peer-reviewed primary research studies that reported on the effects of interventions on the main outcome variable, distress, were targeted. Searches on online databases were conducted using search strategies tailored to each database, combining the main concepts (breast) AND (cancer OR carcinoma OR neoplasm) AND (distress OR emotional distress OR psychological distress) AND (intervention OR program OR treatment) AND (RCT OR randomized controlled trial OR randomized OR clinical trial). Detailed search strategies for each database are presented in Supplementary Table S1.

sec.title: Eligibility criteria
sec.p: For this systematic review, the inclusion criteria were structured around the PICO-SD framework: the targeted population (P) comprised BCSs, which were defined as individuals diagnosed with cancer regardless of the stage in the course of their disease15; the intervention (I) specifically focused on programs explicitly described as managing or reducing “distress”; comparison (C) involved usual care, other psychological interventions, or no intervention at all; the outcome (O) of interest was the quantitative measurement of distress; and the study design (SD) aspect strictly included randomized controlled trials (RCTs). Studies using single-group designs without control groups to verify the effects of immersive simulation education were excluded.

Studies were excluded if they involved cancer types other than breast cancer or did not report interventions specifically targeting distress. Non-randomized study designs—including pilot studies, review articles, case reports, study protocols, and letters to the editor—were also excluded. Additionally, gray literature (e.g., abstract-only publications and theses), studies unavailable in full text, duplicate publications, and non-English literature were not considered.

sec.title: Study selection and search outcomes
sec.p:
Studies were selected following PRISMA guidelines. All database records were imported into Covidence for screening and subsequently exported to EndNote 20 and Microsoft Excel 2019 to support efficient reference management and duplicate removal. After removing duplicate articles, two reviewers independently screened abstracts and titles based on predefined eligibility criteria to ensure consistency and reduce bias. Afterward, they retrieved and screened the full texts of relevant studies. Any disagreements regarding inclusion were resolved through a discussion with a third appraiser (Fig. 1).Fig. 1
PRISMA flow diagram of the literature search. PRISMA, Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses.Fig. 1

sec.title: Quality appraisal
sec.p: The quality of the included studies was evaluated using the Cochrane Risk of Bias 2.0 (RoB 2) tool specifically designed for randomized trials. Studies were classified into three categories based on their risk of bias by using RoB 2: “low risk,” “some concerns,” or “high risk.” This assessment was performed independently by two researchers, and their findings were subsequently combined. When discrepancies in their assessments increased, consensus was achieved through discussion.

sec.title: Data abstraction
sec.p: Data were systematically extracted using a structured template that included study characteristics (author, year, country, participant demographics, sample sizes), intervention details (technology used, program construct/contents, intervention duration, session details, intervention manager), outcome measures (primary and secondary outcomes, distress assessment instruments), and key findings. All data were independently extracted by two researchers, with discrepancies resolved through discussion and consensus to ensure accuracy and completeness.

sec.title: Data synthesis
sec.p: We assessed the feasibility of conducting a meta-analysis; however, substantial heterogeneity across several key dimensions precluded quantitative pooling. The included studies differed in outcome measurement instruments, intervention frameworks and components, delivery modes, and intervention duration and intensity. Additionally, some studies did not report sufficient statistical data for pooling. Under these conditions, a meta-analysis could produce misleading summary estimates. Therefore, we conducted a structured narrative synthesis and calculated standardized effect sizes (Cohen's d) with 95% confidence intervals when the necessary data were available.

sec.title: Search results
sec.p: Fig. 1 presents the PRISMA flow diagram. Overall, 2720 studies published between 2014 and July 2025 were retrieved from the six databases, with 1942 studies remaining after duplicate removal. After title and abstract screening, 1889 records were excluded, leaving 53 for the full-text review. Following full-text review, 39 studies were excluded based on the following: 11 did not evaluate the effectiveness of educational or intervention programs; 7 were not RCTs; 12 did not include distress as a primary outcome; 5 did not exclusively involve BCSs; and 4 were classified as gray literature. Consequently, 14 met the eligibility criteria and were included in the systematic review.

sec.title: Study characteristics
sec.p:
The included studies were all published in English and conducted in various countries (Table 1). Six studies were conducted in Asia–Pacific countries: two in China,16,17 two in South Korea,18,19 one each in Japan20 and Singapore.21 Eight studies were conducted in Western countries: three in the United States,22, 23, 24 one each in Germany,25 Denmark,26 Italy,27 the Netherlands,28 and Belgium.29Table 1
Characteristics of the included studies.Table 1
First author, year, countryNo. of participantsParticipants characteristicsIntervention characteristicsEvidence of efficacy
Mean Age (years)Criteria Related to Breast CancerOthersMain InterventionControl GroupInstrument for Distress MeasurementAssessment timepoints (months unless stated)

BCS, Breast Cancer Survivors; I, Intervention group; C, Control group; QoL, Quality of Life; MSAS-SF, Memorial Symptom Assessment Scale-Short Form; PHQ-9, Patient Health Questionnaire-9; FCR, Fear of recurrence; IES, Impact of Events Scale; CBT, Cognitive Behavioral Therapy; CES-D, Center for Epidemiologic Studies Depressive Scale; MBCT, mindfulness-based cognitive therapy; MBSR, mindfulness-based stress reduction; HADS, Hospital Anxiety and Depression Scale; K-POMS-B, Korean version of Profile of Mood States-Brief; SCL-90, Symptom Checklist-90; SV-POMS, Shortened Version of Profile of Mood States; RSCL, Rotterdam Symptom Checklist.

sec.title: Participants
sec.p: In accordance with the inclusion criteria for this review, the study participants were only those who were diagnosed with breast cancer (Table 1). Among the studies, four focused on patients undergoing treatment,19,24,25,27 four investigated BCSs whose treatment ended,17,21,28,29 and six included all individuals diagnosed with breast cancer with or without current treatment.16,18,20,22,23,26 The total number of participants included in the studies was 2447, and the number of participants in each study ranged from 60 to 617. Only one study16 included younger women with breast cancer under the age of 45 years, with a mean age of 40.2 years, while the majority of studies included all adult women with breast cancer, with a mean age of 48.0–56.3 years.

sec.title: Quality assessment
sec.p:
Fig. 2 illustrates the quality assessment results for the 14 included studies. Of the 14 RCTs, five were scored as “low risk” in all five domains, indicating a low overall risk of bias; two indicated “some concerns” in two domains; and seven indicated “some concerns” in one domain. Overall, all studies demonstrated acceptable methodological quality, with none classified as “high risk."Fig. 2
Risk of bias summary using the Cochrane Risk of Bias 2.0 (RoB 2.0) tool.Fig. 2

sec.title: Intervention characteristics
sec.p:
Interventions varied widely across studies; among them, cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT) was the most common, implemented in five studies,21,24,26,27,29 followed by mindfulness-based approaches in two studies20,25 (Table 2). Regarding delivery format, digital modalities were used in seven studies. Among these, mobile applications were the most common,16,18,25,28 followed by virtual materials17,22 and an internet-based program.23 Face-to-face intervention was applied in only one study,26 while the delivery mode was not explicitly reported in six studies.19, 20, 21,24,27,29 The intervention duration ranged from 3 weeks to 32 weeks. While the number of sessions differed across studies, a fixed number of sessions was not prescribed in all five studies published since 2023,16, 18, 22, 25, 26 instead, participants were allowed to access the intervention on demand. Regarding outcome measurement timepoints, outcomes were assessed at both pre- and post-intervention in six studies.17,18,21,22,27,29 Additionally, outcomes at three timepoints were evaluated in three studies, including follow-up assessments at 3 weeks,19 1 month,20 and 2 months post-intervention.16 Five studies23, 24, 25, 26,28 report four measurement timepoints, with the final follow-up ranging from ≥ 10 weeks to ∼18 months.Table 2
Description of the interventions.Table 2
First authors, yearDelivery modeTheoretical frameworkIntervention componentsDurationSessionIntervention manager
A1Jiang et al., 2024Mobile appPerson-centered care (PCC) frameworkAI-driven personalized content delivery (psychological counseling, coping effectiveness, symptom management, social security via text, image, animation based on questionnaire responses)12 weeksAccess any time as neededNI

Self-monitoring and motivational features (tracking, push notifications, progress feedback)

Daily step tracking with visual feedback and real-time ranking among community members
Community interaction through step count sharing, message posting, and peer encouragement
Weekly motivational messages promoting walking and physical activity engagement
Self-monitoring and social comparison features to enhance adherence and motivation
A6Zhang et al., 2022Virtual realityNIVirtual reality–based CALM psychotherapy integrating immersive experience and guided counseling12 weeks6 separate treatments for 30 min eachPsychologist, oncologist, postgraduates with the certificate of psychological consultant
VR-CALM sessions delivered through audio-visual environments (e.g., seaside, Butterfly Valley) for psychological relaxation
Therapeutic guidance following the CALM manual: Symptom management, self and relationship changes, meaning of life, and future concerns
Verbal reflection and personalized counseling addressing emotional distress and coping strategies
Delivered by trained psychologists and oncologists with weekly supervision to ensure intervention fidelity
A7Ardizzone et al., 2022N/ACognitive–behavioral approachCognitive–behavioral psychoeducational intervention focusing on disease understanding and emotional managementNI50 min per 1 sessionNI
Three individual 50-min sessions addressing breast cancer information, treatment processes, and emotional impact
Structured CBT-based discussions to enhance acceptance, coping, and recognition of psychological responses
Psychoeducation promoting awareness, prevention, and functional strategies for self-management
Individual counseling emphasizing collaboration and patient–therapist transparency within a supportive framework
A8Lally et al., 2020InternetNIWeb-based psychoeducational self-management program12 weeks20–30 min, 2–3 times per weekNI
Five interactive modules incorporating CBT-derived techniques (e.g., cognitive reframing, problem-solving, relaxation) and coping skills training
Survivor video vignettes, reflective journaling and mindfulness exercises, and an online discussion board
Self-paced access with personalized content exploring diagnosis meaning, emotions, body image, communication, and social support
Automated reminders and user analytics to monitor engagement and support independent distress management
A9Park et al. 2020None specifiedNIGroup-based mindfulness-based cognitive therapy (MBCT) program weekly sessions including meditation, gentle yoga, and mindfulness practices (body scan, mindful breathing, walking meditation)8 weeks2 h per week, group formatClinical psychologists, psychiatrists, and nurses with mindfulness training

Compassion and gratitude exercises to enhance acceptance, self-awareness, and positive coping
Daily home practice supported by audio-guided meditation materials and weekly reflection discussions
A10Kim et al., 2018None specified (including Telephonic counselling)NINurse-led psychological intervention7 weeksWeekly, 30 min to 1 h sessions, mix of face-to-face and telephonic counselingCertified oncology nurse with advanced practice training
Individual counseling providing emotional support and cognitive–behavioral guidance through face-to-face and telephonic communication
Psychoeducation on chemotherapy-related symptom management and coping skills for negative emotions, stress, and uncertainty
Body image and self-concept training, expression of fear of recurrence, and use of social resources for problem-solving
Sexual health discussion and self-acceptance exercises to promote emotional recovery and adjustment
Encouragement of proactive coping, self-reflection, and emotional expression to enhance self-control and psychological well-being
A11Visser et al., 2018Tablet-based online appNIBlended group medical consultation combining in-person and tablet-based online support sessions12 weeksGMC: 90 minOnline support group sessions: Three sessions over 3 months, each lasting up to 60 minClinical nurse specialist and medical professional
Face-to-face group consultation led by a medical professional and nurse specialist addressing medical, psychosocial, and survivorship themes
Tablet-based online support group sessions (three video meetings) with clinical nurse specialist facilitation and optional peer interaction
Online resources including educational videos, survivorship information, and secure messaging for patient–professional communication
Reminders and group coordination via shared calendar, email, and technical support to promote participation and continuity

Brief REBT-based cognitive-behavioral sessions teaching identification, disputation, and replacement of unhelpful beliefs
Behavioral strategies for distress reduction including activity scheduling, distraction, and exercise

Posthypnotic reinforcement and self-practice using guided hypnosis and thought record worksheets

Interactive discussions and practice sessions to reinforce self-management and coping strategies

Structured group sessions focusing on anxiety regulation, fear of recurrence, and social support at the start of survivorship
Cognitive-behavioral components emphasizing coping expansion, problem-solving, communication, and cognitive restructuring
Hypnosis training to promote relaxation, self-hypnosis practice, and physiological anxiety regulation
Supportive peer interaction and therapist guidance fostering emotional expression and adaptive coping
Manualized delivery by trained clinical psychologists with regular supervision to maintain fidelity
AI, Artificial intelligence; NI, No information; VR, Virtual Reality; CBT, Cognitive Behavioral Therapy.

sec.title: Primary outcome
sec.p: Only the studies with “psychological” distress as the primary outcome were included. The following tools were used in the studies to measure psychological distress: distress thermometer in four studies;17,18,26,27 hospital anxiety and depression scale (HADS) in two studies;20,29 the Memorial Symptom Assessment Scale-Short Form (MSAS-SF);16 Patient Health Questionnaire–9 (PHQ-9);25 Intrusion subscale of the Impact of Events Scale (IES);22 Rotterdam Symptom Checklist (RSCL);21 Shortened Version of Profile of Mood States (SV-POMS);24 and two28 or three19,23 instruments to measure psychological distress in three studies.

sec.title: Secondary outcomes
sec.p:
Secondary outcomes included quality of life, anxiety, fear of cancer recurrence, and depression (Table 3).Table 3
Comparison of the health-related outcomes of the included studies (N = 14).Table 3
OutcomesA1A2A3A4A5A6A7A8A9A10A11A12A13A14Total

Quality of life✔✔2
Anxiety✔✔2
Depression✔✔2
Self-efficacy✔1
Social support✔1
Decisional conflict✔1
Fatigue✔1
Somnipathy✔1
Concerns about recurrence✔1
Physical distress✔1
Secondary outcomeQuality of life✔✔✔✔✔✔6
Anxiety✔✔✔✔✔5
Depression✔✔✔3
Fear of cancer recurrence✔✔✔✔4

Patient activation✔1
Pain✔1
Health behavior✔1
Body mass index✔1
Need for support✔1
Total weekly steps✔1

sec.title: Effects of interventions
sec.p: Of the 14 included studies, eight (57%) report statistically significant reductions in psychological distress.16, 17, 18, 19, 20,24,25,29 Among these, three studies17,18,29 demonstrate significant effects immediately post-intervention only, three studies19,20,24 show significant effects both immediately post-intervention and at follow-up (3–4 weeks), and two studies16,25 show significant effects only at the 3-month follow-up. A formal meta-analysis was not feasible because of heterogeneity in outcome measures across studies. Instead, standardized effect sizes (Cohen's d) with 95% CIs were reported for each study to enable comparison of intervention effects across trials. However, effect sizes were calculated only for studies with available data, as not all studies reported them. Effect sizes are meaningful only when the compared studies share similar study designs.30 Cohen's d values of ≤ 0.2, 0.5, and ≥ 0.8 indicate small, medium, and large effects, respectively.31

CBT was used in 5 of the 14 studies, making it the most frequent approach to alleviating psychological distress.21,23,24,27,29 However, studies that applied CBT as the primary intervention reported inconsistent results. Two studies24,29 reported significant reductions in distress after the intervention. In Montgomery et al.,24 the effect size was medium to large (Cohen's d = 0.64, 95% CI [0.24–1.04]), while in Merckaert et al.,29 it was small to medium effect (Cohen's d = 0.44, CI not reported). Both studies applied CBT and hypnosis together. Both studies integrated coping strategies alongside hypnosis, demonstrating similar intervention patterns. Furthermore, although neither study specified the delivery mode, the described content suggests the interventions were conducted face-to-face. Meanwhile, Montgomery et al.24 conducted sessions twice weekly for 15–30 minutes over 6 weeks, while Merckaert et al.29 held weekly 2 h-sessions over 6 months. The remaining three studies21,23,27 reported no significant results.

Two studies applying a mindfulness-based approach20,25 reported medium (Cohen's d = −0.54, 95% CI [−0.83, −0.14])25 and medium to large effect sizes (Cohen's d = 1.17, 95% CI [6.35–11.28]), respectively.20 The two studies differed significantly in content and delivery modes. Wolff et al.25 conducted a 12-week mobile app-based study that allowed participants unrestricted access to the intervention via the app without a predetermined number of sessions, time limits, and intervention managers. Meanwhile, Park et al.20 implemented an 8-week group-based intervention conducted weekly for 2 h and delivered by a psychiatrist, psychologist, and nurse. Despite differences in the delivery method, frequency, and intervention duration of mediation, interventions produced consistent results in terms of outcomes.

Jung et al.,18 who applied a peer support approach, reported a significant post-intervention reduction in distress. However, the absence of specific numerical data prevented effect size calculation. Furthermore, this study aims to reduce distress and increase walking steps through the community feature of a tracking app designed to enhance physical activity. However, the study did not specify how the community feature was utilized, nor did it clarify whether participant access to other app features besides the community function was controlled or whether potential bias was addressed. Consequently, drawing clear evidence from the results was difficult.

Zhang et al.,17 examined the efficacy of integrating virtual reality (VR) into the previously validated CALM (Managing cancer and living meaningfully) intervention,32 emphasizing symptom control and communication with others as key intervention components. The results showed a large effect size (Cohen's d = −1.54, 95% CI [−2.05, −1.03]), indicating strong potential for VR integration.

Among the six remaining studies,21, 22, 23,26, 27, 28 none reported significant reductions in psychological distress at any assessment point. These studies included CBT interventions relying primarily on cognitive and psychoeducational strategies,21,23,27 information-focused educational programs,22 and digital platforms with limited structure or professional support.26,28 In contrast to the interventions that resulted in positive outcomes, these approaches were characterized by narrow therapeutic modalities, limited structured implementation, and the absence of clearly articulated theoretical frameworks.

sec.title: Factors influencing intervention effectiveness
sec.p: Across the 14 included RCTs, several factors were associated with moderate-to-large intervention effects. Effective interventions were explicitly grounded in theoretical frameworks, such as cognitive-behavioral principles, mindfulness-based stress reduction, and acceptance and commitment therapy.17,20,24,25,29 Additionally, multicomponent or multimodal designs targeting distress through experiential, physiological, or immersive pathways were employed rather than relying solely on cognitive strategies.17,24,29 These interventions typically involved structured delivery with defined session schedules ranging from 6 weeks to 24 weeks,20,24,29 and professional guidance or therapist support, including in digitally delivered formats.17,20 For example, VR-integrated psychotherapy combined immersive technology with established therapeutic principles,17 mindfulness programs promoted attentional regulation across various delivery modes,20,25 and CBT combined with hypnosis simultaneously targeted cognitive and physiological dimensions of distress.24,29

Conversely, interventions demonstrating limited or inconsistent effects exhibited distinct features. CBT-based interventions focusing primarily on cognitive strategies showed variable outcomes across three RCTs.21,23,27 Other interventions with limited effectiveness included mobile-based peer support programs hindered by weak structure18 and minimally structured approaches without professional support; however, exceptions were observed.25 These findings suggest that intervention content, theoretical frameworks, delivery structure, and professional involvement collectively influence the effectiveness of psychological distress management among BCSs.

sec.title: Intervention effectiveness and implementation factors
sec.p: In this systematic review of 14 RCTs involving 2447 BCSs, 8 (57%) interventions significantly reduced psychological distress, with six showing immediate post-intervention effects and two demonstrating effects only at follow-up. Effective interventions included mindfulness-based approaches, CBT combined with experiential components such as hypnosis, VR-enhanced psychotherapy, and mobile peer support programs. In contrast, interventions relying primarily on cognitive strategies without experiential components, alongside minimally structured programs without professional guidance, demonstrated limited effectiveness. However, substantial heterogeneity in outcome measures prevented the conduct of a formal meta-analysis. These findings offer breast cancer-specific evidence on psychological distress interventions, extending previous reviews11, 12, 13, 14 that examined broader outcomes across diverse cancer types.

Intervention effectiveness varied substantially, with the most successful programs sharing several key features. VR-integrated psychotherapy (VR-CALM),17 mindfulness-based approaches,20,25 and CBT combined with hypnosis24,29 all showed moderate effects despite varying delivery formats and therapeutic focuses. These interventions were unified by their basis in validated theoretical frameworks32,33 and their ability to address distress through experiential or multimodal pathways rather than solely cognitive strategies. For instance, VR-CALM combined immersive technology with established psychotherapy principles, while mindfulness programs promoted attentional regulation across delivery modes. Similarly, adding hypnosis to CBT appeared to improve outcomes by simultaneously targeting cognitive and physiological aspects of distress. These patterns are consistent with prior evidence: Paley et al.11 report mindfulness as a highly effective non-pharmacological strategy for managing distress across cancer populations. Therefore, our findings strengthen this evidence by indicating consistent benefits in BCSs, highlighting mindfulness as a promising approach for oncology distress management.

In contrast, interventions with limited effectiveness exhibited different patterns. CBT was the most commonly used intervention strategy, reflecting its well-established evidence base for psychological treatment.33 However, interventions relying primarily on cognitive and psychoeducational strategies without physiological or somatic components (e.g., hypnosis, body-based techniques) did not demonstrate significant effects.21,23,27 The contrast with effective CBT combined with hypnosis24,29 suggests that integrating cognitive approaches with physiological and somatic pathways potentially enhances effectiveness in addressing the multifaceted nature of distress among BCSs. Similarly, mobile-based peer support interventions showed promise but were limited by minimal program structure and insufficient reporting.18 Overall, the findings suggest that accessible but minimally structured interventions are unlikely to provide reliable benefits without professional guidance and rigorous design. Future research should examine whether technology-assisted or virtual adaptations of multimodal CBT approaches can maintain established efficacy while enhancing scalability.

Beyond therapeutic content, implementation factors emerged as critical determinants of intervention success. Programs delivered with defined session schedules and professional guidance over 6–24 weeks20,24,29 generally produced consistent effects, though implementation format alone did not guarantee success, as shown by a self-directed mindfulness app with medium-sized benefits.25 Studies show that flexible digital programs may struggle with adherence or information overload.34,35 These patterns support broader implementation evidence indicating that digital delivery alone cannot ensure efficacy without structured engagement strategies.36 Thus, successful distress management for BCSs depends on intervention content and delivery strategies that ensure sustained participation and support.

sec.title: Emerging trends, methodological challenges, and future directions
sec.p: This measurement heterogeneity highlights broader conceptual challenges in defining psychological distress for BCSs. Although distress includes anxiety, depression, fear, and emotional discomfort,37,38 limited agreement on its key dimensions has caused inconsistent measurement—a concern also observed by Alanazi et al.9 This variability hinders cross-study comparison and clinical application. Future research should prioritize developing standardized instruments for this population to assess core distress dimensions comprehensively while remaining clinically feasible.

Studies have increasingly used digital and mobile platforms, some incorporating artificial intelligence (AI) for personalized content delivery,16,25 to enhance accessibility and scalability.34,35 However, digital formats showed variable effectiveness, and technology alone cannot guarantee results. Instead, success relied on careful design, validated frameworks, and structured support.36 The most effective digital interventions—VR-CALM17 and mindfulness apps25—integrated technological innovation with evidence-based content and professional guidance. Digital platforms can expand access, but design quality and theoretical grounding outweigh technological features.

Study population characteristics also require consideration. Only one trial specifically targeted young BCSs under 45 years,16 despite their distinct challenges, including disrupted career development, fertility issues, and family planning.39,40 Most studies focused on mid-to-late adulthood (late 40s–50s), limiting evidence for interventions addressing the unique psychosocial needs of younger survivors. Geographically, trials spanned Asia–Pacific and Western regions, supporting generalizability across diverse health care systems. However, cultural adaptation was rarely addressed; only one study explicitly described culturally tailored content for Asians.21 These findings highlight the need for age-appropriate and culturally adapted interventions in future research.

A further concern is the limited use of explicit theoretical frameworks in the design and reporting of interventions. While established psychological approaches, such as CBT33 or mindfulness-based cognitive therapy,20 have been incorporated in some studies, the underlying conceptual models were often not clearly articulated. Theoretical specification alone does not guarantee effectiveness; however, insufficient reporting of the conceptual framework limits the understanding of mechanisms and hinders systematic replication. Theoretical frameworks guide the conceptualization of interventions, enhance intervention coherence, and facilitate identification of active components.41 Without a clear articulation of theoretical frameworks, distinguishing essential elements, replicating programs across settings, and refining approaches based on underlying mechanisms becomes challenging.

Given that psychological distress in BCSs is attributed to complex emotional, cognitive, and contextual interactions, and effective interventions in this review addressed multiple psychological dimensions simultaneously, future interventions may benefit from explicitly grounding their design in comprehensive theoretical models. Frameworks addressing these complexities—such as the Transactional Model of Stress and Coping,42 conceptualizes appraisal and coping processes, and Self-Regulation Theory,43 informing goal-directed behavior change—may be particularly relevant for future research. A clear specification of the theoretical framework would enable rigorous mechanistic studies, facilitate evidence synthesis across studies, and support the development of more targeted interventions for this population.

sec.title: Implications for nursing practice and research
sec.p: This systematic review offers actionable implications for nursing practice and research in managing psychological distress in BCSs. In clinical practice, nurses should incorporate evidence-based approaches shown to be effective in this review, particularly mindfulness-based interventions20,25 and multi-component cognitive-behavioral strategies.24,29 Routine distress screening using standardized instruments—including the distress thermometer—can help identify BCSs who need additional support early.5 When implementing digital interventions, nurses should provide professional guidance and structured program delivery rather than rely solely on self-directed access, as implementation format significantly affects effectiveness.36 Additionally, given the unique psychosocial challenges faced by younger BCSs—disrupted career development, fertility concerns, and family planning39,40—future research should prioritize developing and evaluating age-appropriate intervention strategies for this underrepresented population to establish a stronger evidence base for tailored care. Beyond age considerations, cultural adaptation is also important, particularly in diverse health care settings, where tailored content can enhance engagement and relevance.21

This review highlights some priorities for nursing research. First, developing and validating standardized distress measurement instruments specifically for BCSs would enable meaningful cross-study comparisons and meta-analyses, addressing the heterogeneity identified across studies.9 Second, future interventions should be based on explicit theoretical frameworks from concept analyses of distress,37,38 clearly outlining mechanisms of action to support replication and refinement.41 Third, given evidence that multi-component CBT combined with hypnosis provides consistent benefits,24,29 research should explore adapting these approaches to digital and virtual platforms. The success of VR-CALM17 shows that combining validated therapeutic frameworks32 with immersive technology can enhance accessibility and efficacy, suggesting that hybrid delivery models merit rigorous evaluation in well-designed RCTs. Fourth, cross-cultural validation studies are required to assess intervention effectiveness across diverse populations, particularly given the strong Asia–Pacific representation in current research.44 Finally, implementation research on real-world adoption barriers, cost-effectiveness, and long-term sustainability will be crucial for translating evidence into routine nursing practice.45

sec.title: Limitations and strengths
sec.p: This review has some limitations. First, the review protocol was not prospectively registered on PROSPERO or other public registries, potentially limiting transparency and reproducibility of methodological decisions. Second, substantial heterogeneity in outcome measures prevented the conduct of meta-analysis, limiting the ability to generate pooled effect estimates. Third, limiting inclusion to English-language RCTs may have introduced language and publication bias, potentially excluding relevant studies published in other languages or employing non-randomized designs.

Despite these limitations, notable strengths remain. By focusing solely on BCSs and treating psychological distress as a distinct construct—rather than all cancer types or conflating distress with depression and anxiety—this review offers breast cancer-specific evidence and conceptual clarity, addressing gaps in prior reviews. The comprehensive search through July 2025 captures recent trends in digital interventions, including new AI applications. Geographic diversity (43% Asia–Pacific, 57% Western) improves generalizability across health care systems, while identifying key gaps—measurement heterogeneity, theoretical framework deficits, and underrepresentation of younger survivors—guides future research. Including only RCTs ensures high methodological quality.

sec.title: Conclusions
sec.p: In this systematic review, 14 RCTs specifically targeting psychological distress in BCSs were synthesized. The most effective interventions shared common characteristics, including validated theoretical frameworks and structured, professionally guided implementation. In contrast, cognitive-focused interventions lacking experiential components and minimally structured digital programs demonstrated limited effectiveness, highlighting that technological innovation alone does not guarantee efficacy without careful design and structured professional support. Additionally, younger survivors were significantly underrepresented, while cultural adaptation was rarely addressed. To address these gaps, future interventions should incorporate standardized distress measures, clearly articulated conceptual frameworks, and population-specific approaches. As digital platforms evolve, future research should rigorously evaluate multicomponent delivery models integrating accessible technologies, evidence-based content, and professional guidance to improve distress management in BCSs.

sec.title: CRediT authorship contribution statement
sec.p: H. An: Conceptualization, Investigation, Project administration, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Y. Kim: Conceptualization, Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing - original draft, Writing – review & editing. All authors have read and approved the final manuscript.

sec.title: Ethics statement
sec.p: Not required.

sec.title: Data availability statement
sec.p: Data availability is not applicable to this article as no new data were created or analyzed in this study.

sec.title: Declaration of generative AI and AI-assisted technologies in the writing process
sec.p: No AI tools/services were used during the preparation of this work.

sec.title: Funding
sec.p: This research was supported by the Basic Science Research Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by the Ministry of Education (Grant No. RS-2024-00451448), the Hallym University Research Fund, 2025 (Grant No. HRF-202508-003). The funders had no role in considering the study design or in the collection, analysis, interpretation of data, writing of the report, or decision to submit the article for publication.

sec.title: Declaration of competing interest
sec.p: The authors declare no conflict of interest.
